body_txt: A character must perform an act that is either against their nature or which they have vehemently and constantly protested against doing. Said action can also be just plain humiliating or all of the above—to all parties, perhaps. When this act is aided or witnessed, a character insists that either they must all never speak of this again, ever ; or that a specific person who is guaranteed to ride them never hears of it. Or to put it another way, "What happened here stays here." This trope can be used to introduce a Noodle Incident , with one character reminding the other they promised never to speak of that again. Alternatively, they may have just completed the action, make some passing reference to how unusual or interesting it was, and then pull this line. Subversions can occur when the character(s) kept in the dark reveal themselves to have known all along or if the character who would have used the incident as ammunition, against all expectations, chooses not to do so. Sometimes that somebody will bring it up again, earning the reaction "I thought we agreed to never speak of it again" or something close to that. This often results after someone is Caught in Embarrassing Act . It can be parodied by the person(s) who are told to never mention it again being genuinely unaware of what happened for whatever reason, leading them to ask something like "Speak about what again?", which is then taken by the person wanting the secret kept as a joke implying compliance with their wish. Prequels occasionally resort to this trope as a way to justify their events not being mentioned in works that were written earlier, but set as a later date in-Verse. Will often be Played for Laughs (as the thing that will never be mentioned was usually something funny/embarrassing) or Played for Drama (especially if the event was something that happened between two characters who may or may not end up together ). Compare You Didn't See That , Never Say That Again , That Didn't Happen , May It Never Happen Again , Big-Lipped Alligator Moment , and Deliberately Accepting the Lie .
